title: HSToday Analysis: Post-El Mencho CJNG Crisis Tests U.S.-Mexico Security Cooperation Framework
detail: Homeland Security Today published a policy analysis examining how El Mencho's death and the subsequent CJNG succession crisis are testing the U.S.-Mexico security cooperation framework. The analysis highlights that the February 22 operation relied on intelligence from the U.S. Joint Interagency Task Force-Counter Cartel (JITC-CC), which mapped cartel presence and provided real-time tracking of CJNG movements ahead of the Tapalpa raid. Despite the tactical success, analysts warn that sustained strategic progress requires Mexico to 'take and hold territory formerly ruled by armed groups' rather than conducting decapitation operations. The Trump administration's threats of missile strikes against cartel positions and the FTO designation of CJNG create friction with Mexico's sovereignty concerns even as intelligence cooperation deepens. President Sheinbaum's government, particularly Security Secretary Omar García Harfuch, is navigating the pressure to demonstrate continued operational tempo while resisting direct U.S. military intervention.

media.caption: Post-El Mencho CJNG crisis tests US-Mexico security cooperation framework

title: Rival Criminal Organizations Intensify Pressure on CJNG Across Michoacán and Guanajuato
detail: Ongoing conflict tracking by InSight Crime and ACLED documents continued intensification of violence in Michoacán and Guanajuato as rival criminal organizations test CJNG's weakened post-Mencho cohesion. Carteles Unidos continues operations to drive CJNG out of Michoacán strongholds, while the Santa Rosa de Lima Cartel is pressing in Guanajuato. Security experts warn that CJNG's El Menchito (Rubén Oseguera González) — sentenced to life in prison plus 30 years by a U.S. federal court in March 2025 — is unable to provide leadership from his U.S. prison cell, leaving regional commanders with no clear chain of command. Authorities in Michoacán reported ongoing armed confrontations in multiple municipalities, while Guanajuato remains Mexico's most violent state by homicide count. The International Crisis Group's David Mora noted that CJNG's territorial defense will depend on whether 'El 03' can rapidly consolidate loyalty among disparate regional plaza bosses.

media.caption: Rival organizations intensify pressure on CJNG across Michoacan and Guanajuato
